coredata.dc:title: Nickel nanoparticles decorated on carbon quantum dots as a novel non-platinum catalyst for methanol oxidation; a green, low-cost, electrochemically-synthesized electrocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Javan, Hakimeh
Asghari, Elnaz
Ashassi-Sorkhabi, Habib
Moradi-Haghighi, Masoumeh
coredata.dc:description: The methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) is catalyzed by non- platinum catalysts of nickel nanoparticles (Ni-NPs) that deposited on a modified glassy carbon electrode by reduced carbon quantum dots (RCQD). The cyclic voltammetry (CV), and chronoamperometry (CA) techniques are used for the electrodeposition process. Then the morphology and structure of electrodeposited materials are characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Results showed that Ni-NPs are homogeneously electrodeposited on the surface of CQDs. Also, the catalysis of MOR on electrodes is investigated by different electrochemical techniques like CV and CA, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The electrochemical behavior of electrodes for MOR has been informed with several parameters. For example, for Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE, the peak current density at E = 0.56 V, vs. Ag/AgCl was 32 mA·cm−2 that is higher than of Ni-NPs/GCE. Also, the this catalyst shows excellent stability and high current at constant voltage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrodeposition
Methanol oxidation
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
High active surface area
Carbon quantum dots
Non- platinum catalyst

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of RCQDs and Ni-NPs/RCQDs electrodes
The catalytic performance of the electrodes for methanol oxidation reaction
Effect of potential scan rate on the cyclic voltammetry plots of methanol oxidation
Effect of methanol concentration on cyclic voltammetry plots of Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE
Stability of electrodes by chronoamperometry measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image of (a) and (b) Ni-NPs/GCE and (c) and (d) Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE with different magnifications and their EDX pattern in inset of figure.
Cyclic voltammograms of prepared electrodes in 5 mM Fe(CN)6 and 0.5 M KCl solution at scan rate of 50 mV/s.
Cyclic voltammetry of Ni-NPs/GCE and Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE in (a) 0.5 M KOH and (b) 0.5 M KOH + 0.5 M methanol at scan rate of 50 mV·s−1.
CV profiles for Ni-NPs/RCQD/ GCE in 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M methanol at different scan rates at room temperature.

Logarithmic plot of peak current densities for (a) Peak II of Ni-NPs/GCE at E ~ 0.52 V, vs. Ag/AgCl (b) peak I at E ~ 0.4 V, vs. Ag/AgCl of Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE and (c) peak II at E ~ 0.56 V, vs. Ag/AgCl of Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE electrodes versus scan rate.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE in 0.5 M KOH and different concentration of methanol at scan rate of 50 mV·s−1 and (B) logarithmic plots of current densities of peak II vs. concentration of methanol for (a) Ni-NPs/GCE at E ~ 0.56 V, vs. Ag/AgCl and (b) Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE at E ~ 0.6 V, vs. Ag/AgCl.
Chronoamperometry plots of electrode in 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M methanol at constant voltage of 0.5 V vs. Ag/AgCl.
Nyquist plot of (a) Ni-NPs/GCE and (b) Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE at three different voltages in 0.5 M KOH + 0.5 M methanol.
The values of elements in equivalent circuit fitted in Nyquist diagrams of Ni-NPs/GCE and Ni-NPs/CQD/GCE.

head.ce:title: Nickel nanoparticles decorated on carbon quantum dots as a novel non-platinum catalyst for methanol oxidation; a green, low-cost, electrochemically-synthesized electrocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemistry Research Laboratory, Physical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electrochemistry Research Laboratory, Physical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenyang National Laboratory for Materials Science, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenyang National Laboratory for Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenyang National Laboratory for Materials Science, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) is catalyzed by non- platinum catalysts of nickel nanoparticles (Ni-NPs) that deposited on a modified glassy carbon electrode by reduced carbon quantum dots (RCQD). The cyclic voltammetry (CV), and chronoamperometry (CA) techniques are used for the electrodeposition process. Then the morphology and structure of electrodeposited materials are characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Results showed that Ni-NPs are homogeneously electrodeposited on the surface of CQDs. Also, the catalysis of MOR on electrodes is investigated by different electrochemical techniques like CV and CA, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The electrochemical behavior of electrodes for MOR has been informed with several parameters. For example, for Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE, the peak current density at E = 0.56 V, vs. Ag/AgCl was 32 mA·cm−2 that is higher than of Ni-NPs/GCE. Also, the this catalyst shows excellent stability and high current at constant voltage.

ce:section.ce:para: Methanol is easy to transport and storage due to its liquid nature at room temperature. Its handling is very similar to that of gasoline and diesel fuel. Moreover, it is not contaminated by crude oil; methanol has increased constant mobility and its revival from natural gases and, renewable biomasses is not tricky (Ferdowsi et al., 2015). Oxidation of methanol, as an anodic reaction in direct methanol fuel cells (DMFC), is associated with various byproducts due to its multistage reaction nature. The produced energy density as well as the life of DMFCs, is higher than that of rechargeable batteries; DMFCs have lower operating temperatures compared to other fuel cells; however, they are good alternatives for quick and easy fuel cartridges (Rashidi et al., 2009). Nevertheless, to increase the kinetics and efficiency of the reaction, catalysts such as platinum and its alloys, are used (Adzic et al., 2007; Shao et al., 2007; Liu et al., 2004; Lee et al., 2002). Although CO adsorption on the surface of Pt decelerates the reaction rate, it is still a suitable catalyst, ignoring its high price (Scott et al., 1999). Several excellent and comparable non- Pt catalysts have been introduced within recent decades (Wang, 2005; Bunazawa and Yamazaki, 2009) that are composed of other transition metals, especially the Pd group ones (Metin et al., 2010; Ghasemi et al., 2013; Cao et al., 2010) and nickel based catalysts (Govindasamy et al., 2016; Sakthivel et al., 2018; Govindasamy et al., 2019).

Application of carbonic nanomaterials as a base catalyst is the other approach for more improvement of methanol oxidation reaction and other catalytic reactions. These materials, by providing high surface area, play an essential role in different electrochemical reactions; they consist of carbon nanofibers (Steigerwalt et al., 2001; Hsin et al., 2007); graphene and, graphene oxide (Xu et al., 2008; Antolini, 2012; Li et al., 2010), carbon nanotubes (Wildgoose et al., 2006; Lee et al., 2006; Zhang et al., 2010) and so on. Zhang et al. (Zhang et al., 2012) in an alkaline medium used Ni nanoparticles supported on graphene for the oxidation of methanol. In their work, Ni2+ ions as active species and suitable catalysts in alkaline fuel cells were reduced and anchored with carbon nanoparticles in one pot by using hydrazine. They also applied heat treatment to provide graphene supported Ni (Ni/G) nanoparticles. Ni/G production process was clearly explained; the as-prepared composite of Ni/G also had an excellent electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of methanol in alkaline medium.

As a new kind of nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), are widely used for bio-sensing (Niu et al., 2015; Kong et al., 2012), bio-imaging (Luo et al., 2013; Cao et al., 2007; Cao et al., 2012), drug delivery (Wang et al., 2013); photocatalysis (Li et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2012; Fernando et al., 2015) and electrocatalysis (Tang et al., 2014; Li et al., 2015; Kakaei et al., 2016; Kakaei et al., 2016) in recent years due to their high conductivity, different facile synthesis methods, excellent physical and chemical properties and considerable stability (Lim et al., 2015).

The electrocatalysts supported by CQDs has been applied just in some electrochemical systems. For example, Li and coworkers (Pan et al., 2018) prepared Pt/XC-72-CQDs catalyst by a chemical reduction method and used for MOR. Also; Jahanbakhshi and Habibi (Jahanbakhshi and Habibi, 2016) synthesized Ag-NPs/CQDs nanohybrid for amperometric hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) sensor. The electrochemical investigations showed that the Ag-NPs/CQDs nanohybrid possesses an excellent performance toward the H2O2 reduction. Application of metal nanoparticles based on CQDs in methanol oxidation is limited to just noble transition metals like platinum and palladium.

It this research, Ni-NPs/ RCQD nanocatalyst were synthesized by a facile, controllable and cost-effective electrochemical method; its electrocatalytic activity toward methanol oxidation was investigated in alkaline media with different concentrations of methanol at room temperature. The kinetics of MOR was also studied using electrochemical methods on the fabricated electrodes as catalysts of the reaction.

Nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium hydroxide (KOH), potassium hexacyano-ferrate (III) (K3 [Fe (CN)6]) and methanol were purchased from Merck Co. A three-electrode system consisted of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE), platinum plate and an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl), respectively, as working, counter and reference electrodes were utilized for electrochemical synthesis of Ni and also for electrochemical measurements. The synthesis of CQDs, however, was performed using a two-electrode configuration, which is explained in detail in Section 2.2.

CQDs were prepared by an easy and affordable electrochemical method in a two-electrode cell using the ADAK DC power supply that reported and characterized in our previous study (Javan et al., 2019). Two graphite rods placed parallel to each other with a constant distance of 7 cm, were used as cathode and anode immersed in the 200 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, CQDs were synthesized by exfoliation of graphite anode rod at voltages between 30 and 40 V. The reaction occurred under stirring for five days to obtain a dark- brown homogenous mixture. The mixture was then filtered by slow speed filter paper and centrifuged (Hermle Z 36HK Tabletop high-speed centrifuge) at 22,000 rpm (Zhang et al., 2013; Ming et al., 2012). Scheme 1 illustrates the electrochemical synthesis of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of RCQDs and Ni-NPs/RCQDs electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: For the deposition of prepared CQDs on GCE, the electrochemical route was used. For this purpose, a mixture of KCl (0.10 M) containing CQDs (1.0 mg·mL−1) was prepared; CQDs were dispersed in an ultrasonic bath (BANDELIN SONOREX DIGITEC) for 30 min. The pH of the mixture was then adjusted to 4 by pH meter (Metrohm 654). CQDs were then deposited by cyclic voltammetry in the voltage range of −1.5 to 0.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) with a scan rate of 25 mV/s at room temperature (Javan et al., 2019). In addition, for the deposition of Ni nanoparticles, the constant voltage of −1.0 V, vs. Ag/AgCl was applied for 300 s. A 0.50 M solution of NiCl2·6H2O in 0.10 M KCl was used as an electrolyte for nickel nanoparticles electrodeposition.

The characterization of morphologies and compositions of the electrodes were studied by field emission scanning electron microscopy, FESEM (TESCAN, MIRAIII, Czech Republic), coupled with energy dispersive X-ray, EDX, analyzer. The crystalline structures were investigated by X-ray diffraction, XRD, (Philips model PW1730) with a Cu lamp (λ = 0.1541 nm) operating at 40 kV and 30 mA.

All of the electrochemical experiments were performed using an AUTOLAB PGSTAT30 potentiostat–galvanostat equipped with a frequency response analyzer, FRA, module. Cyclic voltammetry, chronoamperometry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, EIS, tests were used for evaluation of the electrochemical behavior of the electrodes.

For the EIS measurements, the perturbation voltage was 10.0 mV (rms) sinusoidal voltage in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz at different applied potentials. The Nyquist plots were fitted to appropriate electrochemical equivalent circuits using the ZView® (II) software and the corresponding parameters were extracted.

All tests were repeated at least 3 times and the plots of the average results were presented in the manuscript. The only quantitaive data table in the manuscript is the Table 1 that represents the results of fitting of the experimental data to equivalent circuit model and we added the ϰ2(chi-square) for each fitting in the Table. As we know ϰ2 represents the quality of fitting process and should be as low as possible. A chi-square (χ2) statistic is a test that measures how expectations compare to actual observed data (or model results).

The XRD analysis can be employed to confirm the composition and crystalline structure of the obtained materials (Gong et al., 2008; Tehrani and Ab Ghani, 2009). The XRD pattern of the Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE has been shown in Fig. 1 . The broadband at 2θ = 26.7° can be attributed to the (0 0 2) diffraction of RCQD, implying the successful reduction of CQDs during electrodeposition (Wang et al., 2012). In addition, the clear diffraction peaks located at 2θ = 45.8°, 51.8° and 79° correspond to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), and (2 2 0) crystal planes of the face-centered cubic (fcc) Ni particles respectively. The peaks located at 2θ values of 42.4°, 44.3°, 46.4°, 53.2°, and 69.1° are related to the (0 1 0), (0 0 2), (0 1 1), (0 1 2), and (1 1 0) planes indicate the hexagonal closed packed (hcp) structure of Ni (Govindasamy et al., 2016; Niu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020).

So, the presence of RCQDs on the substrate affects the morphology and distribution of the upper layer significantly. It is mainly because of the higher active area performed by RCQDs that affects the current distribution on the electrode surface and causes precipitating the Ni layer with a completely different shape from Ni-NPs/GCE.

The EDX analysis at the inset of Fig. 2 confirms the presence of Ni on the top surface layers. The Ni in high percentage is because of the high surface area RCQD layer available for the deposition of Ni nanoparticles. Also, the high percentage of carbon in Ni-NPs is related to the carbonaceous substrate used for deposition.

The electrochemical behavior of Ni-NPs/GCE and Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE was investigated by cyclic voltammetry to explain their electron transfer processes in the solution of 0.5 M KCl and 5 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] at scan rate of 50 mVs−1 and potential range of −0.1 V to 0.7 V, vs. Ag/AgCl. CV curves in Fig. 3 show a pair of well-defined quasi-reversible anodic and cathodic peaks. As it is seen from Fig. 3, peak potential, Ep, values aren’t changed significantly. While, peak current densities, ip, increase remarkably for Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE compared with Ni-NPs/GCE. These changes are due to the variation of active surface area of the electrodes. So, the Randles- Sevcik equation was used to estimate the approximate active surface area of the electrodes.

The electroactive surface area of the electrodes for a reversible process are calculated by Randles- Sevick equation.
(1)
i p
= 2.99 x
10
5
n A C
D ν
where i p, n, A, C, D, and ν are the anodic peak current density (A·cm−2), number of transferred electrons in the reaction, electroactive surface area (cm2), concentration of the reactant (mol·cm−3), diffusion coefficient of the reactant species and the scan rate (V·s−1), respectively. The redox reaction of K3[Fe(CN)6] involves one-electron transfer (n = 1), and the diffusion coefficient is assumed to be 6.30 × 10−6 cm2·s−1 according to literatures (Jahan et al., 2013; Rao Vusa et al., 2016). So the calculated electroactive surface area for Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE is 0.192 cm2 that is higher than Ni-NPs/GCE (0.113 cm2). It is obvious that the active surface area of Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE is almost 1.5 times higher than Ni-NPs/GCE which is because of the incorporation of RCQDs layer prior to deposition of upper Ni-NPs layer and capacitance current of CQDs (Ensafi et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The catalytic performance of the electrodes for methanol oxidation reaction
ce:section.ce:para:
Once Ni-NPs/GCE and Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE are immersed in an alkaline solution, the nickel nanoparticles are covered with a layer of nickel hydroxide according to Eq. (2) (Weininger and Breiter, 1963; Medway et al., 2006):
(2)
Ni + 2 O H ¯ ↔ N i
O H
2
+ 2 e ¯

Fig. 4 a shows the CV profiles of the prepared Ni-NPs/GCE and Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE electrodes recorded at room temperature in 1 M KOH. On the CV plots in Fig. 4a, two peaks are observed. The peak in the forward scan (peak I) is referred to oxidation of nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) to nickel oxy-hydroxide (NiOOH) that appears typically between 0.35 and 0.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl); NiOOH goes through a reduction at the potentials lower than 0.4 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and this phenomenon appears as a reduction peak in the backward scan on CV plots (peak IV). Eq. (3) indicates the corresponding reactions (Fleischmann et al., 1971).
(3)
P e a k I
a n d
I V
N i
( O H )
2
+
OH
-
↔ N i O O H +
H 2
O + e ¯

It is also clear that both electrodes have the peaks mentioned above, on their CV plots in 0.5 M KOH (without methanol); however, the current densities are much higher for Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE. The peak I current densities for Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE are almost four times higher than those for Ni-NPs/ GCE. It is certainly due to the (i) higher active surface area provided by underneath RCQD layer and (ii) quite different morphology of upper homogenously dispersed Ni-NPs layer on it.

As methanol is added to the solution, significant changes in CV plots occur. For this system, four peaks are observed, peak I and IV appear yet at the same potentials as seen in Fig. 4a. Two new anodic peaks are also observed (peaks II and III in the forward and backward scans in Fig. 4b, respectively). These peaks occur at potentials about 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). The methanol oxidation is represented during forward scan both by the increase in peak current density associated with the formation of NiOOH (peak I) as well as by appearing new peaks (II and III). The peak current densities of peaks II and III are much higher than that of peak I. On the other hand, the peak current density of electro-reduction of NiOOH (peak IV) decreases in the presence of methanol; it is due to a chemical reaction between NiOOH and methanol that diminishes the amount of NiOOH on the electrode surface (Barbosa et al., 2015).

It can be said that at potentials between 0.35 and 0.5 V, Ni (II) species are converted to Ni (III); then, the produced Ni (III) species react with methanol during its oxidation process and are converted to Ni (II). The methanol oxidation, however, occurs incompletely, then, it gets completed by oxidation of intermediates during the reverse scan. Hence, peak III appears due to the later phenomenon (Asgari et al., 2011; Danaee et al., 2008). Eqs. (4) and (5) illustrate these processes as well (Fleischmann et al., 1971; Taraszewska and Rosłonek, 1994; Fleischmann et al., 1972; Vertes and Horanyi, 1974):
(4)
P e a k
I I :
N i O O H + C
H 3
O H → N i
( O H )
2
+ e + i n t e r m e d i a t s
(5)
P e a k
I I I :
N i O O H + i n t e r m e d i a t s → N i
( O H )
2
+ e + p r o d u c t s

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of potential scan rate on the cyclic voltammetry plots of methanol oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: CV profiles were recorded for both electrodes at different potential scan rates (30, 70, 100, and 150 mV·s−1) in a solution of 0.5 M KOH with 0.5 M methanol at room temperature. Fig. 5 represents the profiles acquired for Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE. The peak potentials shifted toward positive values by increases of scan rate mainly because of increased IR drop at high current densities (Hosseini et al., 2018). Peak current densities, i p, were extracted and their changes were evaluated as a function of potential scan rates, ν. Fig. 6 illustrates the logarithmic plots of i p vs. ν as well as the linear regression results and the corresponding linear equations. It can be seen that all curves have shown an almost linear behavior; however, there are some complications; one is the non-zero intercepts and the second issue is the slopes, which are not 0.5 according to Randles-Sevcik equation (Bard and Faulkner, 2001). Generally, as the Randles-Sevcik equation predicts, a completely diffusion-controlled reaction shows the intercept at 0 and the exponent of ν equal to 0.5.

In CV tests and all potential sweep techniques, the potential is continuously changed; so, a charging current, i c, always flows through the system and the overall peak current is the sum of faradaic, i f, and the charging current, i c. While i f is proportional with ν 1/2, i c proportionally relates to ν. In logarithmic plots of i p vs. ν it causes a slope higher than 0.5. If the exponent is more significant than 0.5, then we assume that an additional mechanism of mass transport such as convection or migration is involved. The high amount of charging current’s contribution in apparent i p, can also lead to a slope higher than 0.5. If the exponent is much less than 0.5 , then we usually assume that the analyte is adsorbed on the electrode surface and hinders the corresponding reaction (Asghari and Malekian, 2017; Monk, 2008).

The non-zero intercept of the plots is also originated from different problems like the significant value of IR drop between working and reference electrodes. Sometimes the process is diffusion-controlled, but it is not the only process occurring on the electrode surface. In such cases, the intercept of the Randles-Sevcik plot may also be non-zero and indicate that a non-electrochemical process interferes with the faradaic reaction. Because of the non-electrochemical nature of the mentioned process, it cannot be evaluated by an electrochemical method like CV. This phenomenon may be adsorption of reactants or products and/or intermediates on the electrode surface (Lugovskoy et al., 2010).

From Fig. 7 it is obvious that both Peak I and II show deviations from Randles- Sevcik relationship. In all plots, the slopes (exponent of ν in Randles equation) are far from 0.5. However, it is larger than 0.5 for the plot of i p values of peak I (Fig. 6b), indicating the presence of additional mass transport mechanisms besides diffusion, i.e, convection. The charging of the electrical double layer also introduces a charging current that is capacitive rather than faradaic. For the plots drawn from i p values of peak II for both Ni-NPs/GCE and Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE (Fig. 6a and c), the slopes are far less than 0.5, which can show the footprints of an adsorption phenomenon on the surface. The slope is also much lower for Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE compared with that of Ni-NPs/GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of methanol concentration on cyclic voltammetry plots of Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of methanol concentration on the i p values of peak II on both electrodes was also investigated by recording CV curves. Fig. 7 illustrates the representative plots for Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE. The logarithmic variations of i p vs. C were also plotted and shown in Fig. 7.

As mentioned in Section 3.3, the Randles-Sevcik equation illustrates a linear relation between i p and C with an exponent equal to unity for a completely diffusion-controlled process. So, the logarithmic plots of i p vs. C are expected to be linear with a slope of ca. 1. However, non-zero intercepts, as well as slopes far from unity, were obtained during linear regression. It is evidence of the presence of a non-electrochemical process along with the electrochemical conversions. The most probable one is the adsorption of species on the surface of catalysts.

Another interesting observation is the negative slope of the logarithmic plots for peak II of Ni-NPs/GCE that is entirely unexpected. It means that at high concentrations of methanol, something blocks the faradaic process of methanol oxidation on this electrode. It may be due to the saturation of the less porous surface of Ni-NPs/GCE by adsorbed methanol and/or other species that retain the charge transfer from methanol to the electrode. In fact, the faradaic reaction of methanol oxidation cannot proceed as fast as adsorption and the surface is saturated with adsorbed methanol molecules, but they cannot be oxidized so quickly. Methanol adsorption may also decrease the active surface area of the electrode and lead to lowering the charge transfer rate and corresponding current densities as well.

For Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE the increased active surface area due to the high porosity of granules of Ni-NPs on RCQDs makes a capacity for adsorption of more methanol molecules and the saturation does not occur. So, the faradaic process of electron transfer from methanol to the electrode is not hindered considerably and can take place parallel with the adsorption process. Although the lower than unity slope of the logarithmic plot (Fig. 9b) indicates the retaining effect of the adsorption process on the deviation of electrode behavior from Randles-Sevcik relation for the completely diffusion-controlled process.

A systematic mechanism can be proposed according to the results of CV curves with scan rate and concentration changes:
(6a)

(6c)
( c )

( C

(6d)

I
N i O O H +

(6e)

+ e + i n t e r m e d i a t e s
(6f)

III

(6g)

IV
N i
( O H )
2
+ O
H
-
Eqs. (6a), (6b) and (6c) occur parallel to each other at open circuit conditions just after immersion of the electrocatalysts in the methanol solution (Abdel Hameed and El-Sherif, 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of electrodes by chronoamperometry measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The chronoamperometry, CA, tests were carried out applying a constant potential corresponding to the anodic peak of methanol oxidation (peak II) for each electrode and the results have been shown in Fig. 8 . The increase in the steady-state current density of methanol oxidation on Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE with respect to Ni-NPs/GCE is another evidence of the improved catalytic performance of the electrode in the presence of RCQDs.

In addition, no significant decrease in the steady-state current densities is observed during 3000 s that is evidence of the stability of both electrodes. However, the steady-state current density for Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE occurs later due to the porous structure of the electrode provided by the RCQDs layer on it (Javan et al., 2019; Ashassi-Sorkhabi et al., 2018).

Fig. 9 shows the Nyquist diagrams of Ni-NPs/GCE and Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE recorded at three different voltages of 0.45, 0.50 and 0.55 V, vs. Ag/AgCl in 0.5 M methanol. The Nyquist diagrams consist of two depressed capacitive semicircles at high and low frequencies (Asgari et al., 2011). The depressed semicircle at the high frequency region can be related to the combination of charge transfer resistance and the double-layer capacitance. In the proposed electrical equivalent circuit (shown as inset on Fig. 9) R s, CPE dl and R ct represent solution resistance, constant phase element corresponding to the double-layer capacitance and the charge transfer resistance of methanol oxidation reaction on the electrode, respectively. As we know, in order to obtain a satisfactory impedance simulation when the semicircles are depressed form, it is necessary to replace the capacitor C with a constant phase element (CPE), Q, in the equivalent circuit. It is related to the microscopic roughness, causing an inhomogeneous distribution of activation energies on the surface (Asghari and Malekian, 2017; Maritan and Toigo, 1990).

The values of all parameters obtained from fitting the experimental results to the circuit model and are listed in Table 1. As can be seen from Table 1, R ct decreases by increases of the applied voltages. The n 1 values are also near to 0.5 rather than 1, which indicates that the primary faradaic process is impressed by the limitation in the diffusion of methanol molecules toward the electrode surface. However, since diffusion is not the only process that controls the oxidation reaction, there is no pure diffusional element in the circuit as well as on the Nyquist plots.

As discussed in Sections 3.3 and 3.4 other non-electrochemical limitations, mainly adsorption of methanol and intermediates can be involved. The low frequency semicircle, which is observed due to the adsorption of methanol and intermediates on the electrode surface, consists of an adsorption resistance, R ads, and corresponding CPE, CPE ads. Similar to the R ct, R ads indicate a significant reduction with increasing the applied potential for both electrodes. The overall resistance values, R tot, that are obtained as the sum of the R ct and R ads have also been listed in Table 1. It is found that the R tot values are much lower for Ni-NPs/RCQDs/GCE at all overpotentials. It is related to the increase active surface area of the mentioned electrode because of the effect of underneath RCQDs layer on the morphology of the upper layer of nickel nanoparticles (see Fig. 2). CPE ads and R ads are the elements related to the adsorption of reaction intermediates on the electrode surface.

A facile and green electrochemical route was reported for the synthesis of CQDs, and then they decorated using nickel nanoparticles with high surface area by electrochemical reduction. The obtained catalyst displayed excellent catalytic activity and durability for MOR, after decorating with Ni nanoparticles. The morphology and structure analysis of electrochemically prepared electrodes were studied by SEM, EDX and XRD techniques and results confirmed the accuracy, validity and controllability of this type of synthesis. The prepared Ni-NPs/RCQD catalyst indicated considerable electrocatalytic performance towards the oxidation of methanol. This improved performance was related to the enhanced specific surface area provided by CQDs and healthy interaction between Ni and CQDs that allows faster and easier oxidation of the methanol and superior electron transfer on the Ni-NPs/RCQD/GCE catalyst. The confinement effect of carbon quantum dots can effectively inhibit the aggregation of Ni nanoparticles resulting in an enhanced homogeneity in its morphology and better stability during the operation of the catalyst.

Hakimeh javan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Elnaz asghari: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Habib ashassi-sorkhabi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Masoumeh moradi-haghighi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was carried out in “Electrochemistry Research Laboratory” of “University of Tabriz’’. Authors are grateful for financial supports from Research Office of the University of Tabriz.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Fleischmann, K. Korinek, D. Pletcher, The kinetics and mechanism of the oxidation of amines and alcohols at oxide-covered nickel, silver, copper, and cobalt electrodes, Journal of the Chemical Society, Perkin Transactions 2, (1972) 1396-1403.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Vertes and Horanyi, 1974

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Some problems of the kinetics of the oxidation of organic compounds at oxide-covered nickel electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Vertes, G. Horanyi, Some problems of the kinetics of the oxidation of organic compounds at oxide-covered nickel electrodes, Journal of Electroanalytical Chemistry and Interfacial Electrochemistry, 52 (1974) 47-53.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Hosseini et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ni@ Pd core-shell nanostructure supported on multi-walled carbon nanotubes as efficient anode nanocatalysts for direct methanol fuel cells with membrane electrode assembly prepared by catalyst coated membrane method

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.G. Hosseini, R. Mahmoodi, V. Daneshvari-Esfahlan, Ni@ Pd core-shell nanostructure supported on multi-walled carbon nanotubes as efficient anode nanocatalysts for direct methanol fuel cells with membrane electrode assembly prepared by catalyst coated membrane method, Energy, 161 (2018) 1074-1084.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bard and Faulkner, 2001
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.J. Bard, L.R. Faulkner, Fundamentals and applications, Electrochemical Methods, 2 (2001) 482.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Asghari and Malekian, 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improvement of the electrocatalytic performance of platinum-free hierarchical Cu/polypyrrole/NiOx anode for methanol oxidation via changing the morphology of polypyrrole sublayer by self-assembled pyrrole monomers and overoxidation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Asghari, S. Malekian, Improvement of the electrocatalytic performance of platinum-free hierarchical Cu/polypyrrole/NiOx anode for methanol oxidation via changing the morphology of polypyrrole sublayer by self-assembled pyrrole monomers and overoxidation, Synthetic Metals, 229 (2017) 57-64.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Monk, 2008
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.M. Monk, Fundamentals of electroanalytical chemistry, John Wiley & Sons2008.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Lugovskoy et al., 2010

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Abdel Hameed and El-Sherif, 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave irradiated nickel nanoparticles on Vulcan XC-72R carbon black for methanol oxidation reaction in KOH solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.M. Abdel Hameed, R.M. El-Sherif, Microwave irradiated nickel nanoparticles on Vulcan XC-72R carbon black for methanol oxidation reaction in KOH solution, Applied Catalysis B: Environmental, 162 (2015) 217-226.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ashassi-Sorkhabi et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One step electrochemical route to the fabrication of highly ordered array of cylindrical nano porous structure and its electrocatalytic performance toward efficient hydrogen evolution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ashassi-Sorkhabi, S. Abolghasemi-Fakhri, B.R. Moghadam, H. Javan, One step electrochemical route to the fabrication of highly ordered array of cylindrical nano porous structure and its electrocatalytic performance toward efficient hydrogen evolution, Journal of colloid and interface science, (2018).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Maritan and Toigo, 1990

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On skewed ARC plots of impedance of electrodes with an irreversible electrode process

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Maritan, F. Toigo, On skewed ARC plots of impedance of electrodes with an irreversible electrode process, Electrochimica Acta, 35 (1990) 141-145. .
